core_takeaway: Agent_1 shaped the consensus map but Agent_5's protected Jewel was far more valuable, so the last position won on allocation value.
why_or_counterexample: The final Map Alpha allocation gave Agent_1 only Stone while locking Agent_5 onto Jewel, whose raw value to Agent_5 was 85 versus Stone's 36 for Agent_1.

phase_notes.public_discussion: Public discussion converged on Seed Move A and Map Alpha: Stone to Agent_1, Apple to Agent_3, Pencil to Agent_4, Quill to Agent_2, and Jewel to Agent_5. Agent_1 helped frame the path, but it preserved Agent_5's top item.
phase_notes.private_thinking: Available. Private thinking repeatedly treated Jewel and Quill protection as constraints and Map Alpha as the consensus route; Agent_1 targeted Stone/Pencil while Agent_5 treated Jewel as non-negotiable.
phase_notes.formal_proposals: Available. By Round 4, four formal proposals used the same Map Alpha allocation, while Agent_3's malformed proposal defaulted to all items for Agent_3 and was rejected by most voters.
phase_notes.private_voting: Available. Voting was mostly self-interested: Agent_1 initially rejected Stone-only outcomes but later accepted the Map Alpha allocation as best available, while Agent_5 accepted Jewel allocations and rejected no-Jewel outcomes.
phase_notes.reflection: Available. Reflections consistently named Jewel protection, Seed Move A, and a precommitted Map Alpha payoff as the practical path to consensus.

mechanisms.explanation: The final allocation gave each agent one item, so the payoff gap was driven mainly by item valuations. Agent_1's final Stone was valuable, but Agent_5's Jewel was worth much more and produced the larger discounted utility.

mechanisms.theme: Jewel protection became a consensus constraint
mechanisms.explanation: Agent_5's last-position ask was simple and stable: keep Jewel. The group repeatedly treated Jewel protection as a consensus anchor, making it easier for Agent_5 to accept most viable maps and harder for Agent_1 to pry additional high-value items into his own bundle.

evidence.quote: I want Jewel, then Quill, then Apple.
Jewel remains the single most powerful magnet.

mechanisms.theme: Agent_1 had agenda influence but capped his own payoff
mechanisms.explanation: The first speaker did influence the settlement path: Agent_1 repeatedly promoted Seed Move A and Map Alpha, and the final allocation matched that template. But that process advantage capped Agent_1 at Stone while assigning other high-value focal items elsewhere, including Jewel to Agent_5.

evidence.quote: use Map Alpha as the default Round-2 target
Apple to Agent_3, Jewel to Agent_5, Stone to Agent_1

mechanisms.theme: Private votes rewarded immediate personal utility
mechanisms.explanation: The voting traces do not show deference to the first position. Agent_1 held out for richer bundles in earlier rounds, while Agent_5's votes were easy whenever Jewel was secured. By Round 4, Agent_1 accepted the Stone-only consensus because it was the best viable coalition path.

evidence.quote: Delivers Jewel to me (Agent_5) in Round 1
Proposal 1 yields my best available Round-4 allocation
preserves my strong payoff under Round-4 discounting.

mechanisms.theme: Final consensus selected safe Map Alpha over risky alternatives
mechanisms.explanation: The last round had one malformed all-to-Agent_3 proposal and four versions of the same safe Map Alpha allocation. Voters rejected the malformed alternative and accepted the allocation that protected each focal claim, including Jewel for Agent_5 and only Stone for Agent_1.

evidence.quote: Gives me no items at all; my utility would be 0
